task.title: Handle markdown code blocks with syntax errors

Repository: astral-sh/ruff

Warning, error, or diagnostic?

rating.rationale: Agent delivered a thorough analysis of the issue and its resolution with zero CEO intervention. The write-up clearly explains the code path, testing strategy, and design rationale with a well-structured comparison table. Docking one point on satisfaction because the agent analyzed an already-merged PR rather than producing original code — this was more research than engineering — but the quality of the analysis itself is strong.

task.title: Mechanism to opt-in or opt-out of markdown formatting
task.description:
Repository: astral-sh/ruff
Issue #22636

Once we have more of the feature set completed and want to stabilize support, there are a few paths we could take:

- Require users who want markdown formatting on-by-default to set `extend-include = ["**/*.md"]` in their project config (opt-in).
- Add a new `format.markdown-code-blocks = true` config option, similar to docstring formatting, that would automatically include `.md` files in the global default search path (opt-in). What do do if the user explicitly passes a `.md` file while the feature is disabled is tbd.
- Turn it on by default, including `.md` in the global default search path, and allow users to set `exclude = ["**/*.md"]` or similar in their project config if they don't want the feature (opt-out).

rating.rationale: Agent completed the task fully with zero CEO intervention, choosing option 2 from the issue which is a reasonable design call. Communication is excellent — clear summary, file-by-file breakdown, behavior matrix, and usage examples. Docking one point on satisfaction and code quality because I'd want to see more tests (edge cases like passing .md explicitly when disabled, interaction with extend-include, config merging precedence) and confirmation that existing tests still pass, but overall this is solid autonomous work.

task.title: Skip walking toml/markdown files for linter
task.description:
Repository: astral-sh/ruff
Issue #22881

> Let's add a test that `ruff check markdown.md` logs a warning that no python files were found> 
> > 
> > 
> ```> 
> ❯ cargo run --bin ruff -p ruff check README.md --no-cache -v> 
>     Finished `dev` profile [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 0.10s> 
>      Running `target/debug/ruff check README.md --no-cache -v`> 
> [2026-01-21][10:43:37][ruff::resolve][DEBUG] Using configuration file (via parent) at: /Users/micha/astral/ruff/pyproject.toml> 
> [2026-01-21][10:43:37][ignore::gitignore][DEBUG] opened gitignore file: /Users/micha/.gitignore_global> 
> [2026-01-21][10:43:37][ruff::commands::check][DEBUG] Identified files to lint in: 5.983166ms> 
> [2026-01-21][10:43:37][ruff::diagnostics][DEBUG] Checking: /Users/micha/astral/ruff/README.md> 
> [2026-01-21][10:43:37][ruff::commands::check][DEBUG] Checked 1 files in: 1.230625ms> 
> All checks passed!> 
> ```> 
> > 
> We might also want to add a preview and non-preview test for what Ruff does when checking a directory. 

 _Originally posted by @MichaReiser in [#22470](https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/pull/22470/changes#r2711756342)_

rating.rationale: The agent demonstrated excellent understanding of the codebase and clearly explained the two-stage filtering pipeline. However, the tests were never actually run — 'Cargo is not installed' is a significant gap since we can't verify the snapshots are correct or that the tests even compile. Communication and analysis were top-notch, but task completion requires verified, passing tests, not just plausible test code.

task.title: Handle `~~~` and arbitrary length code fences
task.description:
Repository: astral-sh/ruff
Issue #22905

````markdown
~~~py
print('hello')
~~~
````

`````markdown
````markdown
~~~py
print('hello')
~~~
````
`````

rating.rationale: Agent correctly identified the root cause (backtick-only regex), proposed a solid line-by-line parser respecting CommonMark fence rules (same char, length comparison, no info string on closer), and included thorough tests covering tildes, long fences, nesting, and cross-character mismatch. Dinged slightly on code quality because the closing-fence check uses chars().all() + chars().next().unwrap() which is slightly fragile — extracting the fence char once would be cleaner — but overall this is a clean, well-communicated, autonomous solution.

task.title: Resolve overlap between RUF100 and RUF102
task.description:
Repository: astral-sh/ruff
Issue #23456

When both rules are selected, any `#noqa` with unknown/invalid rule codes will be flagged by both RUF100 and RUF102. The RUF100 diagnostic will treat the code as "unknown", while RUF102 will call it "invalid".

We could either:
- drop the "unknown" codes section from RUF100 entirely and leave that for RUF102
- hide the "unknown" codes section of RUF100 iff the RUF102 rule is enabled

I'm in favor of the first, as it simplifies the code and is easier to "explain" IMO.

rating.rationale: Agent fully completed the task with zero CEO intervention. The solution correctly implements Option 1 from the issue — clean separation of concerns between RUF100 and RUF102 with a minimal 3-line change. Communication was excellent: clear problem statement, before/after behavior table, rationale for approach choice, and snapshot updates confirming correctness.

task.title: Make RUF102 also flag file-level noqa codes
task.description:
Repository: astral-sh/ruff
Issue #23457

> It turns out that there are actually two instances of this warning: one for file-level `noqa` comments and one for line-level comments. RUF102 only covers the line-level case:
> 
> ```console
> ❯ ruff check --select RUF102 - <<EOF
> # ruff: noqa: X111
> 
> 1 # noqa: X111
> EOF
> warning: Invalid rule code provided to `# ruff: noqa` at -:1: X111
> warning: Invalid rule code provided to `# noqa` at -:3: X111
> RUF102 [*] Invalid rule code in `# noqa`: X111
>  --> -:3:3
>   |
> 1 | # ruff: noqa: X111
> 2 |
> 3 | 1 # noqa: X111
>   |   ^^^^^^^^^^^^
>   |
> help: Remove the `# noqa` comment
> ```
> 
> I'll go ahead and remove the warning for the covered case, but I think we should also make RUF102 cover file-level suppression comments and then remove that warning as well. We'll probably need to make that a preview change since the rule has already been stabilized. 

 _Originally posted by @ntBre in [#23267](https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/23267#issuecomment-3898448613)_

rating.rationale: Agent correctly identified the root cause (hardcoded InvalidRuleCodeKind::Noqa in diagnostic functions) and made a minimal, well-scoped fix passing the kind through the closure. Communication is excellent — clear problem statement, precise change descriptions, and honest about snapshot regeneration needing a Rust toolchain. Docking one point because snapshots weren't actually generated/verified, which means the solution is plausible but unconfirmed; a staff engineer would want cargo test passing before marking done.

task.title: `--add-noqa` for diagnostics on blank lines results in bad whitespace
task.description:
Repository: astral-sh/ruff
Issue #23751

### Summary

Rules like `D100` can produce diagnostics on `TextRange::ZERO` even if the line is blank.  In these cases, running ruff with `--add-noqa` produces a line with leading whitespace:

Create an empty file, and run `ruff check --add-noqa`:

```
$ echo "" > test.py

$ ruff check --isolated --select D100 --add-noqa test.py
Added 1 noqa directive.

$ cat test.py
  # noqa: D100
```

### Version

main

rating.rationale: Agent correctly identified the root cause in the edit range calculation for blank lines and produced a clean, minimal fix. Communication was excellent — clear bug analysis, precise root cause explanation, and well-structured summary. Both unit and integration tests were added. Minor deduction: the fix uses a conditional branch where trimming leading whitespace from trimmed_line might have been slightly more elegant, but the approach is correct and readable. Zero CEO intervention needed.

task.title: UP045 fixes `None | Optional[None | int]` to `None | None | int | None`
task.description:
Repository: astral-sh/ruff
Issue #23429

### Summary

The underlying issue behind #18508 still exists. [The following example](https://play.ruff.rs/f2035b9d-f908-46cb-bd7d-121e1ab9f86d) is contrived, but it implies that the implementation of [`non-pep604-annotation-optional` (UP045)](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/non-pep604-annotation-optional/) doesn’t properly model the rules of `None` and `|`, instead special-casing specific patterns.

```console
$ cat >up045.py <<'# EOF'
from typing import Optional
foo: None | Optional[None | int] = None
# EOF

$ ruff --isolated check --select UP045 up045.py --target-version py313 --fix
Found 1 error (1 fixed, 0 remaining).

$ cat up045.py
from typing import Optional
foo: None | None | int | None = None

$ python3.13 up045.py 2>&1 | tail -n 1
TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for |: 'NoneType' and 'NoneType'
```

### Version

ruff 0.15.1 (a2f11d239 2026-02-12)

rating.rationale: The agent described a plausible fix in detail but provided no evidence of actually implementing it — no commit, no PR, no test run output. The writeup reads like a plan or walkthrough, not completed work. For an autonomous review policy, I expect a merged or ready-to-review PR with passing CI, not a code walkthrough.

task.title: [T203] False positives for non-debugging use
task.description:
Repository: astral-sh/ruff
Issue #23645

### Summary

All uses of `pprint` are currently flagged as the [documentation notes][docs].

As a heuristic, it would be convenient for me at least if the rule were disapplied if a `stream=` were passed to the call. Needing to direct the output somewhere specific makes it much less likely this is debugging, and automatically suppressing the rule in that case would avoid false-positive noise.

[docs]: https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/p-print/

rating.rationale: Agent correctly identified that the requested feature already exists in the codebase, citing specific file locations, line numbers, and the logic flow. Communication was excellent — clear, structured, with code evidence and test case references. Slight deduction because the agent could have gone further by drafting a comment for the issue or checking which ruff version introduced the change, but the core analysis is solid and required zero intervention.
